g.data-narration: Packet switching breaks data into small chunks called packets that travel independently through the network.
g.text: A communication method where data is broken into small chunks called
packets, transmitted independently through the network without a dedicated path.
Key: Statistical multiplexing, dynamic routing, shared resources

g.data-narration: Each packet has three parts: header with addresses, payload with data, and trailer for error checking.
There are two main types of packet switching: Datagram (connectionless) and Virtual Circuit (connection-oriented).
In datagram switching, each packet is independent with complete destination address. No connection setup is required.
g.text: Connectionless: Each packet contains full destination address and is routed
independently. No prior connection setup needed. Packets may take different paths.

g.data-narration: See how 4 packets from A to D take different paths: P1 via R1→R2, P2 via R3, P3 via R4, arriving out of order.
g.text: 4 Packets sent from A to D - Each packet routed independently (may arrive out of order)

g.data-narration: Datagram offers fast setup with no delay but packets may arrive out of order with no QoS guarantee.
Virtual circuit establishes a logical path before transmission. All packets use VCI instead of full addresses and follow the same route.
g.text: Connection-oriented: A logical path (VC) is established before data transfer.
Packets carry short VCI (Virtual Circuit Identifier) instead of full address.

g.data-narration: There are two types: Permanent Virtual Circuits (PVC) are manually configured and permanent; Switched Virtual Circuits (SVC) are dynamically established.
g.text: Dynamic setup on demand, then terminated

g.data-narration: Virtual circuit ensures ordered delivery with QoS support but requires setup time and fails completely if any link breaks.
Key differences: Datagram needs no setup and routes each packet independently; Virtual circuit requires setup but guarantees order.
The Internet uses IP as datagram service at network layer, while TCP adds connection-oriented reliability at transport layer.
g.text: Internet combines both approaches: IP (datagram) at network layer + TCP (reliable) at transport layer

g.data-narration: When a link fails: Datagram reroutes packets dynamically, Virtual circuit fails entirely and needs re-establishment.
Remember: Datagram is connectionless with independent routing; Virtual circuit is connection-oriented with guaranteed delivery.
g.text: • Packet switching breaks data into independent packets with header/payload/trailer
• Datagram (IP/UDP): Connectionless, per-packet routing, no QoS, resilient to failures
• Virtual Circuit (X.25/Frame Relay/ATM): Connection-oriented, fixed path, QoS support

g.data-narration: For exams: Remember IP is datagram, X.25/Frame Relay/ATM are virtual circuit. VC guarantees order, datagram is more resilient.
